source: Gaze Glass — A Family of Mortals (2).docx
heading: 1 Marok
paragraphs: Marok floated three-feet above psychedelic fox nip in The Realm of Paradise.

He dove into the gold grass and rubbed the sides of his furry face against silky magenta flowers, inhaling the aroma of roses and vanilla with bright yellow eyes—the whites turning pinker by the second. The intoxicated fox licked sweet pollen off his dark snout with his lime-green tongue and cackled as silver dragonflies raced across the blushing sky, leaving behind shimmering trails of pearl dust. Cool wind lifted lilac butterflies circling a cluster of glittering blue lotus floating on iridescent water—and white elk with bumpy antlers made of diamond ran between twisted cherry blossom trees.

Yesterday, Marok had graduated from The Academy of Fate and Destiny at the top of his class with a permit to command lesser spirits and the sacred seal to design Judgments. One week ago, he accepted a coveted divineship under a deity. When he heard his ruler was forged from the light that created the first flames, he whimpered and ran in concentric circles with his white bushy tail between his back legs.

A gold-faced peacock with an alabaster beak descended into the fox nip field and screeched when it landed beside Marok. The fox yelped before shooting up into the air, sneezing green flames onto the fox nip. He shrieked as a wave of fire began to spread. Quickly, the peacock flapped its wings, snuffing out the emerald embers within seconds. “Intoxicated before your divineship?” the peacock inquired.
Marok raised his velvety tail high and hiccuped, “I’m enjoying myself before the big day.”
“Today is that big day,” the peacock corrected.
“You’re hilarious—” the fox started to say.
“I did not come to make jokes . . . you foolish fox!” the peacock interrupted.
“The God of Beauty sent me on behalf of The God of War!” the peacock added.
Marok blinked, fell onto his back and cackled. “You almost had me there, Natana! Can you believe it has only been a week since we graduated?”

Seconds after, the peacock named Natana rattled its black wings and the golden eyes on each feather blinked. A pressure formed in the fox’s throat as the wind faded and the swaying fox nip became still. “Your boss is furious. You are late!” Natana screeched. “Go to Utopia!”
Marok hiccuped. “Natana, during judgment design—” the fox started to say.

“Go or you’ll perish and do not bore me with your questions!” the peacock shrieked. They propelled their thick wings forward, casting a gust so powerful it lifted the fox from the sparkling soil. Marok yelped and kicked the air. He conjured wicked, white flames between tufts of emerald green. The delirious fox covered his glazed eyes with his little paws, snickered, and then hiccuped. He wasn’t worried. After all, he completed an internship in the underworld assisting a high priestess of the dead. Between sorting souls at judgments—horrific periods of malice and mayhem carried out by divinely appointed mortal families to honor the gods—and training the gods’ chosen to wield divine power, he developed a work ethic that he was certain would please his assigned ruler.

“Good Luck!” Natana shouted before Marok vanished, leaving behind a puff of shimmering green smoke. The cloud split apart into thin wisps, scattered in every direction, snaking between trees. As Marok disappeared, his fluffy figure reappeared in wisps two dimensions above—in Utopia—the new home of the gods. The frantic fox landed on a bed of cold flowers made of clear quartz; the sound of petals shattering pierced the silence. Quickly, he hoisted himself into the air. “MASTER! I AM HERE!” Marok shouted. “PLEASE FORGIVE ME!”

Twenty feet ahead, the God of War floated in their primordial form—swirling black and crimson smoke. They peered through the dimensions through a floating mirror and watched Earth with disgust, ready to scorch the planet into dying embers.

Marok removed his tail from between his legs and took a centering breath. The pressure in his chest eased at the aroma of honeysuckle. The fox bowed to The God of War, and lowered his plush ears to cover his eyes. “How many times have we told the demigods and mortals? Do not build their homes high on Earth. The weight is too great for Earth to bear,” the God of War seethed. “And they obscure the mirror I use to watch humanity.”

The fox didn’t look at the cluster of skyscrapers with their overseer. Instead, he unfolded his ears and shifted his gaze to a group of purple butterfly spirits fluttering above his head toward a bright Indigo sun snug in the inky sky.
Marok’s eyes twinkled at the sparkling gemstone dust rising from the green field and he inhaled the sweet aroma of moonflower water and pink lotus wine.

The God of War growled at the fox. He did not tolerate being ignored. Eight black eyes with crimson pupils emerged from the grim orb. Each eye faded, then reappeared above the butterflies. One by one, their wings were set ablaze. Heat waves spread over the fox’s face. Thin green hair rose from the tips of Marok’s tall ears; he shuddered as white ash fell on his wet snout, prompting a shrill sneeze. Marok grimaced and said, “They also do not worship you.”
“Tsch. Don’t remind me,” the God of War said, before turning back to the mirror.
They roared, generating a wave of malice upon Earth—earthquakes shook the finest cities, toppling skyscrapers upon mortals. Oceans churned and swallowed ships for lunch.
“You’re loud and insolent,” the God of War said, melting gemstone flowers all around.

With only intention, the deity could annihilate entire cities, and the fox fled, fearing it would be next. Before the God of War could incinerate Marok, its consort appeared between the two. The God of Beauty revealed themselves as a bright, magenta haze. Within seconds, they transformed, the light molding to mimic the appearance of a human woman with long, silky hair that shone as bright as Earth’s sun. Her skin was softer than silk, and her magenta eyes were bright as light, shimmering like unpolished rose quartz. Though petite, her ten arms—five on each side— had the power to destroy universes. “Is this correct? Is this truly what they look like?” the God of Beauty asked. Marok cackled and nodded. “Yes, my goddess.”

The God of Beauty waved her arms over her head and frowned. “Look at me! I’m a helpless mortal!” Marok cackled. Eight black eyes with crimson pupils appeared above the fox. “Let him prove himself,” the Goddess said. “It’s his first attempt at weaving a Judgment.” The God of Beauty snapped her icy fingers and tore the air in front of Marok. The fox flew through the rift and reappeared from a portal inches away from the goddess’ sharp nose. “Tell us . . . have you found our chosen?” the God of Beauty asked. “Chosen? Oh … yes!” Marok said. “A broken family. The eldest siblings may have similarities, but one is a temperamental warrior and the other is a bitter scholar that craves control. The youngest moves like lightning and has a mind that is very malleable.”

“I’ve seen it before,” the God of War growled, red eyes appearing inches from his face. Marok stiffened. “Wait! There is one more. A middle child, but he should not be trusted with any boon. There is far too much grief in his heart and chaos in his mind. He wouldn’t be able to shed his ego. He’s far too self-destructive. Please, the other three are entertaining and will make fine pledges!”
“Good. I am in need of quality entertainment,” the God of Beauty sang, her right palm under the floating fox. “The last million judgments haven’t quenched my thirst. I want a good laugh!”
“Oh! What’s funny about the people of Earth is that they—” Marok started.
“Do I look like I care about humor?” the God of War interrupted, radiating heat akin to forest fire. Marok jumped out of the goddess’ arms and bowed, his tail between his legs.
“The answer is no,” the God of Beauty whispered with a sidelong glance.
“No?” Marok whimpered. “The answer is no!”
Putrid red smoke rolled out from the grim orb. “Tsch . . . present your report.”
“But today is my first day!” Marok cried. Thirty red eyes appeared above the fox.
“War!” the God of Beauty warned. “Let him prove himself.”

The God of War scoffed. “Ugh! Prepare your designs for the gods, and I will say this once—do not make a fool of me.” Marok nodded in response. “No one could make a fool out of you, War. When have we ever put on a terrible show?” the God of Beauty asked with a honeyed voice. “Tell me. Is it even possible to make a fool out of us?”
“This world has divine artifacts,” the God of War said coldly. “There is too much risk.”
“I’ve accounted for those!” Marok cried and cleared his throat. “There are twelve artifacts that can disrupt my glorious machine, but they are nearly unattainable by mere mortals. ”
“Nearly is an opportunity for defiance!” the God of War boomed. The God of Beauty pulled the fox into an embrace. “Tell me. How could a mortal utilize an artifact?”
“They can tamper with the Scales of Judgment,” Marok quickly said. “If successful, they can reap untold havoc across countless parallel universes.”
“Very good. Now tell me … what must be done prior to a judgment?”
“First, I must find souls who will pledge themselves to you.”
“Then?”

“I take them to locate a spirit guide who will train them for the Judgment!” Marok said and grinned. “Sweet fox. You are forgetting one important detail,” the goddess warned. Marok gaped and his eyes bulged. “Oh! I call for my ruler to offer a sacred seal to the pledge.”
“And what is the pledge?” the God of War asked coldly.

“An unbreakable contract between the divine and a chosen mortal to undergo divine transformation to deliver judgment on behalf of the gods. By pledging loyalty with their life’s thread, they receive boons from their ruler.” the fox replied and sneezed green embers. “During the judgment—the pledges must shed their egos by overcoming deadly trials designed by The God of Justice. ”
“And?” the God of Beauty asked louder.
Marok cleared his throat. “Well— pledges who shed their egos become demigods. The mortals—the ones who possess shards of mercy—will be sorted into a new caste system.”
“See!” the God of Beauty said with a honeyed voice. “He’s ready.”
The fox smiled and his tail wagged, purple butterfly spirits landing on his furry head. “Do not worry! Your temples will have more shards than any other!”
“I will make an example of you if we do not reign supreme,” the God of War warned.
“Do not fret, mister fox. You will also report to me!” the God of Beauty said in a singsong voice. “Since when?” the God of War asked.
“Since now.”
Marok looked away.
“Very well. You will be his executioner should he fail,” the God of War declared.

The God of Beauty winked at the fox and raised her ten hands near the God of War’s fluid form. Upon her touch, The God of War morphed to the shape of a human man, with a jawline sharp enough to cut any stone. His biceps were bigger than any of Earth’s men. His body was like a statue, sculpted with perfect symmetry. She combed her sharp fingers through his raven hair, and he smiled before pulling her onto his chest. Their gemstone lips touched, and lightning bolts spread from their faces, revealing sacred geometry, their embrace radiating cosmic energy. For a brief moment, their bodies fused. They metamorphosed again as they kissed, the God of War took on the biology of a human woman, and The God of Beauty took the shape of a human man. The God of Beauty floated backward and laughed.
“Remember . . . hatred is just love in excess. We both know you don’t love them! Why brute?” the God of War did not move a single celestial atom. “Because it is my nature?”
Marok hiccuped. “There is something I should mention about the siblings. They were given—”
“Would you like to visit that planet?” the God of Beauty asked The God of War sweetly.
Between creating and destroying universes, the Gods answered and rejected prayers.

“We could punish them or–” the God of Beauty began coldly. “Come on. Let’s have some fun!” The God of War peered into their mirror and saw mortals praying for help. A young farmer in India hid with his wife in a barren room. Malnourished, she placed her chapped fingers on his bony cheeks for one final kiss.

Men outside slammed their fists on the wooden door, demanding entrance and money. Two of them circled the house. They found a large black tarp on the ground covered with green coconuts and freshly plucked orange and yellow marigold flowers.

The men shouted profanities and laughed. Then I picked up the juicy fruit and hurled it at the window. The second man jumped onto the tarp. As he stomped the farmer’s harvest, the farmer’s wife began to sob. “The eldest have the power to—” Marok tried to say.

“One blessing!” the God of War growled before answering the farmer’s prayer by touching the mirror with a dark claw, possessing the mortal’s body. The farmer’s eyes became murderous, black pupils spilling malice into the whites of his eyes. He turned toward his wife’s teary eyes and placed a single finger between them. Inky lines spread over her face. “Come, my love. Let’s enjoy ourselves,” he cooed.

A wave of light scattered across the dimly lit room from the farmer’s wife’s forehead, illuminating a somber home. The windows with metal bars allowed slivers of light and the red brick walls bore tall cracks. The gray concrete floor was cold as ice and adorned with woven mats made of hay and a small, black desktop computer sat under a velvety oil painting of an elephant. The woman’s deep, brown eyes rolled back in her head, and just as the door caved in, rose quartz eyes replaced them. The God of War stared into the God of Beauty’s eyes as the horde rushed in. The eyes they had known and loved since the beginning of all things. Eyes that could never be transformed. Green tendrils of smoke collected near the dirty ceiling. Marok fell from the green cloud and hit his head on the cold floor. “Yuck!” the God of Beauty spat through the farmer’s wife’s lips. “Monkeys!”
“You didn’t bless this one, my love,” he reminded her.

The gang gaped at the fox. Marok yelped and became invisible, skittering across the sticky floor. Seconds after, the mortals pointed their guns at the couple; The God of War and The God of Beauty stood and embraced. Then they laughed before facing the men. One of them, a merchant, stepped forward and reached for the God of Beauty’s thin neck with a sly smile, but before he could touch her, she faded and vanished. The humans gasped and looked at each other. She crawled across the ceiling like a spider above them. Within seconds, her head split in half and transformed into the resemblance of a praying mantis.

The God of War closed his eyes and sneered. He knew what she was capable of, and of what these men were incapable of. Mortal weapons could not scratch her. Fire could not warm her. Though she did not possess the God of Word’s speed, she was too quick for mortals to touch.

The men heard her before they saw her. “Touch a girl without permission and she has every right to kill you,” she whispered, and then dropped. As she descended, her arms morphed into scythes. She severed the first man’s arms, and then his legs. Blood pooled under his limbs. The God of Beauty licked the edge of her scythe and moaned in ecstasy. “ Oh! War! Their fear is delicious. You must have a taste!”

The mortals tried to flee, but the God of War was outside waiting for them. “She has a big appetite,” he said. “It’s best if you stay for dinner.” The men raised their rifles and fired. Bullets fell on dry, cracked soil. The God of War flared his nostrils and raised a hand. The men grasped their thick necks and their feet lifted from the earth. They pleaded through tears and gasps for breath. “Do you know what I detest?” the God of War asked, tossing one man into a nearby house with the flick of a finger. “You think everything belongs to you.” He growled and catapulted the rest of them toward the sun. “You belong to us.” A black cobra emerged from the bushes, slithered up the God of War’s vessel, and draped itself around his neck. He caressed its dark scales until his consort emerged from the house. “The prayer has been answered,” she said. A second serpent appeared and made its way up to the God of Beauty’s neck.
“If only they’d bend the rules,” she exclaimed before kissing her consort’s cheek.
“We must leave these vessels. Perhaps they would let us stay?” she added.
The God of Beauty released her control of the farmer’s wife as she spoke. The serpent opened its mouth and swallowed a magenta haze.
“Let us meet our chosen.” The God of War pointed to the unconscious farmer. “And them?”

“They leave trash upon the earth and eat animals without honor,” the God of Beauty replied and hissed. “They’re disposable.” The God of War nodded, and possessed the cobra, leaving the farmer on the ground next to his wife.

They began to regain consciousness. The serpents peered into each other’s dark beady eyes, bit the humans’ necks, and slithered away, scaly necks entwined. The farmer and his wife may have had their prayers answered, but they didn’t know how indifferent gods are to mortals.
